epigraph.text: He has made everything beautiful in its time. He has also set eternity in the human heart.

content.text:
Regular polygons—figures with all sides equal and all angles equal—have 
captivated mathematicians and artists for millennia. From the hexagonal 
cells of honeycombs to the octagonal stop signs on our streets, these 
shapes combine perfect symmetry with practical utility. In this section, 
we develop a unified formula for the area of any regular polygon.

content.title: Regular Polygon
content.content:
A **regular polygon** is a polygon that is 
both equilateral (all sides congruent) and equiangular (all angles congruent).

content.text:
Examples include the equilateral triangle (3 sides), square (4 sides), 
regular pentagon (5 sides), regular hexagon (6 sides), and so on.

Every regular polygon has a **center**—a point equidistant from 
all vertices. This symmetry is key to our area formula.

content.title: Center and Radius
content.content:
The **center** of a regular polygon 
is the point equidistant from all vertices. The **radius** 
is the distance from the center to any vertex.

content.title: Apothem
content.content:
The **apothem** of a regular polygon is the 
perpendicular distance from the center to the midpoint of any side.

content.text:
The apothem is always shorter than the radius (except for a circle, 
where they're equal). The radius goes to a vertex; the apothem 
goes to the middle of a side.

content.title: Central Angle
content.content:
The **central angle** of 
a regular $n$-gon is the angle formed at the center by two adjacent radii:
\[
 \theta = \frac{360°}{n}
\]

content.text:
Here is the key insight: any regular $n$-gon can be divided into 
$n$ congruent isosceles triangles by drawing all radii from the center 
to the vertices.

items: Base = one side of the polygon (length $s$)

content.text:
Since there are $n$ such triangles, the total area is:
\[
 A = n \cdot \frac{1}{2}sa = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (ns) \cdot a = \frac{1}{2}pa
\]
where $p = ns$ is the perimeter of the polygon.

content.title: Regular Polygon Area
content.content:
The area of a regular polygon with apothem $a$ and perimeter $p$ is:
\[
 A = \frac{1}{2}ap
\]
Equivalently, if the polygon has $n$ sides of length $s$:
\[
 A = \frac{1}{2}a \cdot ns = \frac{1}{2}ans
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Area of a regular hexagon
content.problem:
A regular hexagon has side length 8 cm and apothem $4\sqrt{3}$ cm. 
Find its area.
content.solution:
First, find the perimeter:
\[
 p = 6 \cdot 8 = 48 \text{ cm}
\]
Then apply the area formula:
\[
 A = \frac{1}{2}ap = \frac{1}{2}(4\sqrt{3})(48) = 96\sqrt{3} \approx 166.3 \text{ cm}^2
\]

content.title: Finding the apothem
content.problem: A regular pentagon has side length 10 and area 172.05. Find the apothem.

content.text: Relating Apothem, Radius, and Side Length

For a regular $n$-gon, the apothem $a$, radius $r$, and side length $s$ 
are related through trigonometry. Consider one of the $n$ isosceles 
triangles formed by two radii.

The apothem bisects the central angle $\theta = 360°/n$, creating 
a right triangle with:

items: Other leg = half the side length, $s/2$

content.title: Finding dimensions of a regular hexagon
content.problem: A regular hexagon has radius 10. Find its side length and apothem.
content.solution:
For a hexagon, $n = 6$, so the half central angle is $180°/6 = 30°$.

The side length:
\[
 s = 2r \sin 30° = 2(10)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = 10
\]

The apothem:
\[
 a = r \cos 30° = 10 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 5\sqrt{3} \approx 8.66
\]

(Note: For a regular hexagon, the side length equals the radius!)

content.title: Area of a regular octagon
content.problem:
A regular octagon is inscribed in a circle of radius 12. Find the 
area of the octagon.
content.solution:
For an octagon, $n = 8$, so the half central angle is $180°/8 = 22.5°$.

First, find the side length:
\[
 s = 2r \sin 22.5° = 2(12)(0.3827) \approx 9.18
\]

The apothem:
\[
 a = r \cos 22.5° = 12(0.9239) \approx 11.09
\]

The perimeter:
\[
 p = 8s \approx 8(9.18) = 73.44
\]

The area:
\[
 A = \frac{1}{2}ap \approx \frac{1}{2}(11.09)(73.44) \approx 407.3
\]

content.text:
For an equilateral triangle with side $s$, we can derive a direct 
area formula. The height $h$ of an equilateral triangle is:
\[
 h = \frac{s\sqrt{3}}{2}
\]

Therefore, the area is:
\[
 A = \frac{1}{2}bh = \frac{1}{2} \cdot s \cdot \frac{s\sqrt{3}}{2} = \frac{s^2\sqrt{3}}{4}
\]

content.title: Equilateral Triangle Area
content.content:
The area of an equilateral triangle with side length $s$ is:
\[
 A = \frac{s^2\sqrt{3}}{4}
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Equilateral triangle
content.problem: Find the area of an equilateral triangle with side length 12.

content.text:
A regular hexagon can be divided into 6 equilateral triangles. 
If the hexagon has side length $s$, each triangle has area 
$\frac{s^2\sqrt{3}}{4}$, so:

content.title: Regular Hexagon Area
content.content:
The area of a regular hexagon with side length $s$ is:
\[
 A = 6 \cdot \frac{s^2\sqrt{3}}{4} = \frac{3s^2\sqrt{3}}{2}
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Regular hexagon area
content.problem: Find the area of a regular hexagon with side length 6.

content.title: Stop sign
content.problem:
A standard stop sign is a regular octagon with side length 12.5 inches 
and apothem 15.1 inches. Find its area.
content.solution:
The perimeter is $p = 8 \cdot 12.5 = 100$ inches. The area is:
\[
 A = \frac{1}{2}ap = \frac{1}{2}(15.1)(100) = 755 \text{ in}^2
\]

content.title: Honeycomb cells
content.problem:
Honeycomb cells are regular hexagons with side length approximately 
3 mm. Find the area of one cell.

marginNotes.text:
The outer sanctuary had a rectangular 
doorframe, and the one at the front of the Most Holy Place was similar.

The city was laid out like a square, 
as long as it was wide. He measured the city with the rod and found 
it to be 12,000 stadia in length.

The inner sanctuary was twenty cubits 
long, twenty cubits wide and twenty cubits high.

God saw all that he had made, and 
it was very good.
